function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing defines MAU and daily posts separately. What specific caveat accompanies each metric, and what do the caveats imply regarding the reliability and interpretability of AI engagement metrics?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about a SpaceX S-1 filing that defines MAU (Monthly Active Users) and daily posts metrics, and what caveats accompany each. Let me retrieve information from the filing.

The filing URL is: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Let me first parse the HTML page and then retrieve information about MAU and daily posts definitions and caveats.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

arguments.prompt: What is the definition of MAU (Monthly Active Users) and daily posts in the filing {{spacex_s1}}? What caveats or qualifications accompany each metric? Please quote the exact language used in the filing for both definitions and their caveats.

results.content:
Based on the filing's Preamble glossary, here are the exact definitions and accompanying caveats for MAU and daily posts:

**Monthly Active Users (MAU)**

*   **Definition:** "“MAU” (or monthly active users) refers to the total number of users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement (“active users”)."
*   **Methodology for Combined MAUs:** "In presenting combined MAUs across the two platforms, we seek to identify and account for users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic so that such users are not double-counted when measuring MAU. Furthermore, only users who have registered for an X or Grok account are included."
*   **Caveats/Qualifications:** "While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period. We track the subset of users who used Grok’s AI features and those who have not based on the source of their server requests."

**Daily Posts**

*   **Definition:** "“daily posts” on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok."
*   **Caveats/Qualifications:** "This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."

steps.reasoning_content: I have the information I need. Let me also check if there's any additional context about AI engagement metrics and their reliability. Let me search for more context on these metrics.

arguments.prompt: In the filing {{spacex_s1}}, what additional context is provided about the reliability, limitations, or interpretability of MAU and daily posts metrics? Are there any risk factors or notes about how AI-generated content affects these metrics? Please quote relevant language.

results.content:
# Reliability, Limitations, and AI-Generated Content Affecting Daily Posts Metrics

Based on the provided S-1 preamble chunks, the most direct and relevant language regarding the interpretability of the "daily posts" metric—and how AI-generated content affects it—appears in the **definitions section**:

> **"daily posts" on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok. This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI.**

This definition is significant for several reasons:

1. **Explicit acknowledgment of AI-generated content inflating the metric**: The phrase *"This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI"* is a direct interpretability caveat. It signals that the daily posts figure is not a pure measure of human user activity and could be materially affected by automated or AI-driven content generation.

2. **Conflation of human and AI activity**: The metric aggregates both human user posts *and* Grok's real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities into a single volume figure, making it difficult to distinguish organic human engagement from AI-assisted or AI-generated output.

3. **Use as a competitive differentiator**: Later in the preamble, the company cites this metric as evidence of Grok's strength:

> **"With approximately 350 million daily posts, X enables freshness, relevance, and contextual awareness for Grok that we believe is a competitive differentiator."**

This means the same metric that is acknowledged to potentially include AI-generated content is also being used to substantiate the platform's competitive positioning—creating a tension between the metric's marketing utility and its reliability as a measure of genuine human discourse.

---

## Absence of MAU-Specific Caveats

Notably, **none of the provided chunks contain a definition of "Monthly Active Users" (MAU)** or any explicit risk factor discussing how AI-generated content affects MAU reliability. The provided text only addresses the "daily posts" metric in this regard.

---

## Related Risk Context

While not framed specifically as a metric-reliability risk, the broader risk factor language about AI outputs being flawed or misused provides indirect context for why AI-influenced metrics could be unreliable:

> **"AI technologies, the models, algorithms, prompts and datasets on which they rely, and the recommendations, forecasts, analyses or other output that such AI technologies assist in producing, may be flawed, insufficient, of poor quality, rely upon incorrect, inaccurate, harmful or illegal data, reflect unwanted forms of bias, hallucinate, misrepresent, mislead or contain other errors or inadequacies, any of which may not be easily detectable."**

> **"certain of our AI products, such as Grok, have been alleged to be susceptible to 'data poisoning' in the past."**

These statements, while focused on output quality rather than metric integrity, underscore that AI-generated content contributing to the daily posts volume may itself be unreliable or manipulated—further complicating the interpretability of the aggregate metric.

---

## Summary

The S-1 preamble provides **one explicit note** on how AI-generated content affects the daily posts metric (the definition stating it "may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI"), but offers **no parallel caveat for MAU** and does not include a dedicated risk factor specifically addressing the distortion of engagement metrics by AI-generated content. The closest supporting context comes from general AI output-quality risk disclosures.

steps.reasoning_content: I have enough information to answer the question. Let me also verify the MAU caveat by retrieving more specific information about it.

arguments.prompt: In the filing {{spacex_s1}}, please find and quote the exact language about MAU methodology, including any statements about duplication, estimation, or limitations. Also quote any language about how users are tracked between Grok and X platforms.

results.content:
Based on the provided filing chunks, here is the exact language regarding MAU methodology, duplication, estimation, limitations, and user tracking:

**MAU Methodology, Duplication, Estimation, and Limitations:**
> • “MAU” (or monthly active users) refers to the total number of users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement (“active users”). In presenting combined MAUs across the two platforms, we seek to identify and account for users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic so that such users are not double-counted when measuring MAU. Furthermore, only users who have registered for an X or Grok account are included.
>
> While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period. 

**Tracking Between Grok and X Platforms:**
> We track the subset of users who used Grok’s AI features and those who have not based on the source of their server requests.

steps.message: I have sufficient information to answer the question. Let me submit my final answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
I now have all the information I need to answer the question. Let me compose a comprehensive answer.

The question asks:
1. What specific caveat accompanies each metric (MAU and daily posts)?
2. What do the caveats imply regarding the reliability and interpretability of AI engagement metrics?

For MAU:
- Definition: Total number of users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement
- Caveat: "While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period."
- Implication: MAU is an estimate, not a precise count. There may be residual double-counting between Grok and X users that the deduplication methodology doesn't fully capture. The metric is approximate.

For Daily Posts:
- Definition: Aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok
- Caveat: "This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."
- Implication: The daily posts metric conflates human activity with AI-generated content. It's not a pure measure of human engagement. The metric could be inflated by AI-generated content, making it difficult to interpret as a measure of genuine human discourse.

Combined implications for AI engagement metrics:
- Both metrics have caveats that limit their reliability as measures of pure human engagement
- MAU is acknowledged as an estimate with potential residual duplication
- Daily posts explicitly includes AI-generated content, blurring the line between human and AI activity
- The metrics are used to substantiate competitive positioning (e.g., "approximately 350 million daily posts" cited as a competitive differentiator), creating tension between marketing utility and reliability
- The broader risk factor language about AI outputs being flawed, susceptible to data poisoning, etc., further complicates the interpretability of these metrics

Let me submit my final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Caveats on MAU and Daily Posts in the SpaceX S-1

The SpaceX S-1 preamble defines both engagement metrics and attaches a distinct caveat to each. The caveats point to different — but related — reliability problems for AI engagement metrics.

### 1. MAU (Monthly Active Users)

**Definition (as disclosed):**
> "MAU" (or monthly active users) refers to the total number of users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement ("active users"). In presenting combined MAUs across the two platforms, we seek to identify and account for users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic so that such users are not double-counted when measuring MAU. Furthermore, only users who have registered for an X or Grok account are included.

**Specific caveat:**
> "While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period."

**Implication for reliability/interpretability:**
- MAU is explicitly characterized as an **estimate**, not a precise count.
- The company acknowledges that its cross-platform deduplication methodology (based on sign-in traffic) **"may not fully capture all instances of duplication,"** meaning a single human user accessing both Grok and X could still be counted more than once.
- The metric is also restricted to **registered users only**, so unregistered/lurker engagement is excluded — meaning MAU understates total reach while potentially overstating unique reach due to residual double-counting.
- Net effect: MAU should be read as a directional approximation of unique human reach across the combined Grok + X estate, with an undisclosed (and possibly material) margin of error.

### 2. Daily Posts

**Definition (as disclosed):**
> "daily posts" on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok.

**Specific caveat:**
> "This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."

**Implication for reliability/interpretability:**
- The daily posts figure is **not a pure measure of human engagement**. It aggregates (a) human-authored X posts and (b) Grok's AI-generated interactions, analysis, and generative outputs into a single volume number.
- The caveat explicitly concedes that the count **may include AI-generated posts and AI-managed accounts**, so the headline volume can be inflated by non-human activity that the company does not separately quantify or disclose.
- This is particularly significant because the same metric is used elsewhere in the filing as a competitive differentiator — e.g., "With approximately 350 million daily posts, X enables freshness, relevance, and contextual awareness for Grok that we believe is a competitive differentiator." The metric is simultaneously (i) acknowledged to potentially include AI-generated content and (ii) cited as evidence of platform strength, creating a tension between its marketing utility and its reliability as a measure of genuine human discourse.

### Combined Implications for AI Engagement Metrics

Taken together, the two caveats imply that the filing's headline AI engagement metrics are **directional rather than precise**, and that they **conflate human and machine activity in ways the company does not fully resolve**:

1. **MAU** is an estimate with acknowledged residual double-counting risk — its reliability as a unique-human-reach figure is qualified.
2. **Daily posts** is an aggregate that may include AI-generated content and AI-managed accounts — its reliability as a measure of organic human engagement is qualified, and it cannot be cleanly decomposed into human vs. AI contributions from the disclosure alone.
3. Neither caveat is quantified. The filing does not disclose the magnitude of residual duplication in MAU, nor the share of daily posts attributable to AI generation or AI-managed accounts. Investors therefore cannot independently assess how much of the reported engagement is human-driven.
4. The broader risk-factor language in the filing — noting that AI outputs "may be flawed … hallucinate, misrepresent, mislead or contain other errors" and that Grok has been "alleged to be susceptible to 'data poisoning'" — reinforces that AI-generated content contributing to the daily posts volume may itself be unreliable or manipulated, further complicating interpretation of the aggregate metric.

In short: MAU is disclosed as an **approximation with deduplication limitations**, and daily posts is disclosed as a **volume figure that may be inflated by AI-generated content**. Both caveats limit the metrics' usefulness as clean indicators of genuine human engagement with the company's AI products, and the absence of quantification means readers must treat the headline numbers as management estimates rather than audited, decomposable engagement data.

  "sources": [

      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX) Form S-1, filed May 20, 2026 — Preamble definitions of MAU and daily posts, and related risk-factor language"
    }
  ]
}
